title: Chapter 1: The End Starts with a Low Battery
content: Roxanne Vance's phone died at 6:47 AM.
Not the battery. The whole thing. Screen went black mid-scroll, and when she tried to restart it, the Apple logo flickered once like a dying firefly and never came back.
"You've got to be kidding me."

She was in the Fine Arts building's ground-floor studio, surrounded by half-finished charcoal pieces and the smell of turpentine. She'd pulled an all-nighter finishing a piece for her final portfolio — a deconstructed interpretation of Botticelli's Venus, except Venus was wearing platform boots and holding a black card instead of a scallop shell.
Prescient, in retrospect.
The overhead fluorescents buzzed. Then they didn't.
Darkness.
Roxanne stood very still for a count of ten, waiting for the emergency lights to kick in. They didn't. The building had gone from fully lit to absolutely black in under a second.
"Okay."
She fumbled for her desk drawer, found her emergency lighter — a sleek matte-black one she'd bought because it matched her aesthetic, not because she expected to use it — and flicked it on.
The small flame revealed her studio in fragments: the easel with Venus's unfinished face, the stack of sketchbooks, the cold latte she'd abandoned hours ago. The turpentine catch can. Her bag.
She grabbed her bag, stepped into the hallway, and immediately wished she hadn't.
The hallway windows showed the sky.

It wasn't morning anymore. It wasn't any color the sky was supposed to be. The horizon had turned a deep, bruised violet-black, shot through with veins of something that looked like lightning but moved wrong — too slow, too deliberate, like roots growing through the atmosphere.
"Okay, that's not normal."
Screaming started somewhere on the second floor.
She ran toward the sound. Not because she was brave — because the art building had exactly one exit on this floor, and the screaming was coming from that direction.

Students spilled out of the stairwell. Some were crying. One guy was trying to use his phone as a flashlight, apparently not having accepted that phones were dead. A girl in pajamas was hyperventilating against the wall.
"What happened?" Roxanne grabbed the nearest person — a lanky illustration major she recognized from the shared critique sessions. "Jake. What happened?"

Jake's face was the color of wet paper. "There's something in the quad. Something — I don't know — it's got a chainsaw. But the chainsaw is, like, part of it. It crawled out of the ground and just — started."
"Started what?"
"Taking people."
Roxanne's brain, trained by years of art school critique sessions where you had to stay calm while someone eviscerated your thesis, clicked into a strange, cold focus.
"How many exits?"
"What?"
"From the building, Jake. How many ways out?"
"Front doors. Back door by the loading bay. And —" He swallowed. "The basement tunnel to the old chapel. But nobody's gone that way."
"The chapel tunnel it is."
She was already moving. Jake followed because he didn't know what else to do.
The basement stairs were darker than the rest of the building. The emergency lighter's flame seemed smaller here, like the darkness was eating the light before it could spread.
"Roxanne."
She stopped.
A familiar voice. Coming from the top of the stairs.
Marcus.
Her boyfriend of two years. Pre-med. Polo shirts. A smile that said *I'm the responsible one* while carefully never being responsible for anything that cost him.
He was standing at the top of the basement stairs, silhouetted against the faint violet glow from the hallway windows. Behind him, two other students she didn't recognize.
"Marcus. Thank god. We need to get out —"

"There is no out." His voice was strange. Controlled. Like he was about to deliver a presentation. "The quad's full of them. The back lot too. The only way anyone's getting out of this building is if something distracts them."
Roxanne felt the cold before she understood what he was saying.
Distracts them.
"Marcus. What are you saying?"

"It's about the group, Roxanne. You said it yourself last semester — the group is more important than the individual. Well, this is that moment. If you go out there, it'll buy us enough time to get to the parking structure."
"If I —" She stopped. Laughed. A short, disbelieving sound. "You want me to be the distraction."

"I'm not saying I *want* it. I'm saying it's the logical choice. You're an art major, I'm pre-med. After the apocalypse — and yes, this is an apocalypse — medical skills are worth more than drawing skills. That's just math."

"That's just —" Roxanne stared at him. In the flickering lighter flame, his face was perfectly composed. He'd already decided. He'd made peace with it. "You've been thinking about this since you saw the sky, haven't you?"
"Roxanne."
"You've been ranking people by usefulness. Calculating who to trade."
"It's called survival."
"It's called being an asshole."
She turned to go back up the stairs. She'd take her chances with the chainsaw thing. At least it was honest about wanting to kill her.
She made it three steps before Marcus grabbed her arm.
"I'm sorry."
He shoved her.
Not hard enough to knock her over — just hard enough to make her stumble backward. Off balance. Her heel caught on the edge of a basement step.
She fell.
The lighter flew out of her hand. The flame died.
And somewhere in the dark, a wet metallic scraping sound started.

Roxanne hit the basement floor hard enough to feel her teeth click together. The impact jarred through her wrist, her elbow, her shoulder. For a few seconds she just lay there, tasting blood from where she'd bitten her cheek.
The scraping sound was getting closer.
She couldn't see anything. The basement was absolute dark — not the comfortable dark of a room with your eyes closed, but a thick, hungry darkness that pressed against her skin.
She pushed herself up. Her palm landed on something wet. She didn't want to know what.
*Think. Think.*
But there was nothing to think about. She was in a dark basement. Something with a chainsaw was coming. Her boyfriend had just thrown her at it like a piece of meat.
She was going to die in a basement, wearing yesterday's eyeliner and an oversized denim jacket, without having finished her Botticelli piece.
That was when the card appeared.
She felt it first. A warmth in her palm, like someone had pressed a hand warmer into her grip. Then a weight — solid, familiar, like a credit card, but thicker. Heavier.
She looked down.
A black card lay in her hand.

It wasn't plastic. It wasn't metal either. It was something between — obsidian-smooth, catching light that didn't exist in this dark basement. The edges glowed with a faint, deep violet luminescence, the same color as the sky outside.
And on the back, in letters that looked like they'd been etched by something that existed before humanity learned to write:
*Bearer of the Primordial Void. Balance: ∞.*
Roxanne stared at it.
She had approximately twenty-eight dollars in her bank account before the world ended. She was two months behind on her credit card. She'd been surviving on instant ramen and spite.
And now she was holding an infinite black card.
"What."
The scraping sound stopped.
She looked up.
The Chainsaw Executioner stood ten feet away.

It was tall — seven feet, maybe eight. Its body was wrapped in what looked like blood-stained bandages, but the bandages moved, shifting and writhing like they were alive. Where its face should have been, there was a rusted iron mask with a single vertical slit. And where its right arm should have been, a chainsaw grew out of its flesh — not held, not attached, but *grown*, metal teeth fused with bone and sinew.
The chainsaw wasn't running. But it was dripping.
The Executioner tilted its head. The mask's slit focused on her.
And Roxanne heard a voice in her head — not a sound, but a transmission, like a text message being typed directly into her brain:
*SUMMONING CONTRACT ACTIVE. TARGET: FEMALE, EAR EARLY TWENTIES, DARK HAIR. PRICE: 50,000 SOULS.*
She blinked.
Wait.
She could see the price. Floating above the Executioner's head like a restaurant menu item, visible only to her. 50,000 souls. Someone had paid to have her killed.
Below that, in smaller text:
*CONTRACT HOLDER: UNKNOWN. REMaining duration: 47 years, 8 months.*
*BUYOUT PRICE: 50,000 SOULS. TRANSFER PRICE: 100,000 SOULS.*
Roxanne looked at the card in her hand.
She looked at the Executioner.
She looked back at the card.
"Okay," she said, her voice steadier than she had any right to be. "Let's see if this thing works."
She held up the card.
*Purchase: Transfer contract of the Chainsaw Executioner. Price: 100,000 souls.*
The card pulsed once. Warm.
*APPROVED.*
The Chainsaw Executioner stopped moving.
Its head tilted the other way. The rusted mask seemed to... reconsider.
Then it lowered its chainsaw arm. Dropped to one knee. The wet metallic scraping sound that had been following Roxanne since she fell was now the sound of it kneeling on concrete.
*CONTRACT TRANSFERRED. NEW HOLDER: ROXANNE VANCE. DURATION: 100 YEARS.*
Roxanne exhaled. She hadn't realized she'd been holding her breath.
"Okay. Okay. That's — that's a thing that happened."
She looked down at the kneeling monster, then up the stairs where Marcus had disappeared.
"Hey."
The Executioner raised its head.
"Remember the guy who shoved me down here?"
A pause. Then a slow, deliberate nod.
"Good. Let's go have a conversation."
Marcus was halfway across the quad when he heard the scraping sound behind him.
He turned.
Roxanne was walking out of the Fine Arts building. The Executioner followed three paces behind her, chainsaw dragging along the ground, carving a thin groove into the asphalt.
She was covered in dust and a little bit of blood from where she'd bitten her cheek. Her eyeliner was smudged. She looked like she'd crawled out of a horror movie.
She looked magnificent.
"Roxanne. I —"
"Don't."
She held up the black card. It glowed faintly in the violet light of the apocalypse sky.
"You sold me for fifty thousand souls. Fifty thousand. Do you know what I can buy with that?"
She didn't wait for an answer.
"I just bought *him*. The thing you sent to kill me. One hundred thousand souls. Transfer. He's mine now."
She stepped closer. The Executioner stepped with her.
"So here's the thing, Marcus. The apocalypse has a new currency. And I have infinite of it."
She smiled. It wasn't a nice smile.
"You're a debtor now. And I'm the only one who can clear your tab."
She turned to the Executioner.

"New contract. Your first task as my employee: make sure Marcus Chen survives the next twenty-four hours. No matter what. Keep him alive, follow him everywhere, and make sure he knows exactly what he traded away."
The Executioner's chainsaw roared to life for the first time. The sound was deafening, a mechanical scream that echoed across the empty quad.
Then it stopped.
The Executioner turned to face Marcus.
And followed.
An hour later, Roxanne stood in the middle of Blackwood University's main quad, surrounded by the scattered remnants of what had been her life.
The sky was still wrong. The monsters were still out there. She could hear distant screams and the occasional sound of something breaking.
But the immediate area around the Fine Arts building was quiet. The Executioner had cleared it before following Marcus.
She looked at the card again.
*Balance: ∞.*
"What are you?" she whispered.
The card didn't answer. But the sky flickered, and for just a moment, she felt something vast and ancient turn its attention toward her. Not hostile. Curious.
Like she'd just introduced herself to the universe.
She pocketed the card and walked back into the Fine Arts building. There were people hiding in the studio on the second floor. Lena was among them.
Time to buy a university.
*End of Chapter 1*
